title: Nitrogen-doped W0.75Re0.25 Superconducting Nanowire Single Photon Detectors
authors: F. Colangelo, Abhishek Kumar, H. Wang, F. Avitabile, C. Attanasio, I. Esmaeil Zadeh, C. Cirillo
abstract: Nitrogen-doped Tungsten-Rhenium superconducting alloys were recently proposed as a promising material platform for superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs), offering a favorable balance between high normal state resistivity and tunable superconducting properties. In this work, we report on the fabrication and characterization of SNSPDs based on ultrathin W0.75Re0.25 films deposited by reactive DC magnetron sputtering in a mixed Ar/N2 atmosphere. Meander detectors with 70 nm linewidth exhibit saturated internal detection efficiency up to 1310 nm at 2.5 K, with sub-nanosecond rise times, decay times of the order of a few nanoseconds, and timing jitter of 73.2 ps measured with room temperature amplifiers.
summary: This work demonstrates the development of Nitrogen-doped WRe superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors. The new material platform provides high detection efficiency at telecom wavelengths and very low timing jitter. This advancement offers a versatile alternative to existing NbN and WSi-based detectors for quantum technologies.
main_problem: The need for new material platforms for Superconducting Nanowire Single-Photon Detectors (SNSPDs) that combine high detection efficiency, low timing jitter, and the ability to operate at elevated temperatures for quantum communication and sensing.
main_result: Nitrogen-doped W0.75Re0.25 SNSPDs achieve high internal detection efficiency (up to 100% at 1060 nm and 95% at 1310 nm) with a low timing jitter of 72.3 ps and sub-nanosecond rise times.
method: The researchers used reactive DC magnetron sputtering to deposit ultrathin WRe films in an Ar/N2 atmosphere, followed by electron beam lithography and reactive ion etching to create 70 nm linewidth meander detectors.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of ultrathin (10 nm) amorphous Nitrogen-doped W0.74Re0.25 superconducting thin-film meander detectors operated at cryogenic temperatures (2.5 K).
key_observables: Internal detection efficiency (IDE), timing jitter (FWHM), rise and decay times, critical current (Ic), critical current density (Jc), and electron-electron/electron-phonon interaction times.
important_parameters: Tc approx 5.22 K, linewidth 70 nm, pitch 140 nm, jitter 72.3 ps, and wavelength range 660-1310 nm.
main_assumptions: The study notes potential performance limitations due to resistivity inhomogeneities from partial oxidation and early-stage fabrication processes.
figures_summary: Figure 3 shows single SNSPD pulse decay with an exponential fit; Figure 4 displays the timing jitter distribution with a Gaussian fit.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material motivation, details the fabrication and sputtering process, presents superconducting and electrical characterization, reports photon detection performance and temporal dynamics, and concludes with an analysis of interaction times and optimization needs.
why_interesting: This paper is relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it presents a new material platform for high-performance single-photon detectors, which are essential components for quantum communication and photonic quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Normalized R(T) for N2-doped (violet) and pure (magenta) 10-nm WRe films with Tc(d) as an inset; (b) R□(d) of the two samples (same color scheme) at 10 K.

FIG. 2: (a) V(Ib) characteristic of two N2-doped WRe SNSPDs (linewidth of w = 70 and 100 nm) with inset showing the SEM picture of the w = 70 nm detector; (b) normalized photon count rate of the same 70-nm linewidth N2-doped WRe SNSPD as function of Ib.
FIG. 3: Single SNSPD pulse data points (violet dots) of the 70-nm linewidth device, with exponential fit of the decay (magenta line); characteristic times are also reported.
FIG. 4: 70-nm linewidth SNSPD timing jitter data points (violet dots), with Gaussian fit (magenta line) and resulting FWHM.
